comments.text: A computer search disproves $\max_{n\leq x}f(n)=\max_{n\leq x}F(n)$ being true for all $x$. Take $x=2\cdot 3\cdot 5\cdot 7=210$, then $$f(210)=2^7+3^4+5^3+7^2=128+81+125+49=383.$$ One can easily verify that indeed no value of $n\leq 210$ gives a larger value for $f(n)$. But now consider the admissible choice $$(a_1,a_2,a_3,a_4)=(2^7,3^3\cdot 7,5^3,1)=(128,189,125,1)$$ which are pairwise coprime, each at most $210$, and all of whose prime factors lie in the set of prime divisors of $210$, namely $\{2,3,5,7\}$. Then, $$F(210)\geq 128+189+125+1=443,$$ so $$\max_{n\leq 210}F(n)\geq 443>383=\max_{n\leq 210}f(n).$$

Thanks! Interesting. (Although I'm not sure that 1 should be allowed...otherwise strictly speaking you could take lots of copies of 1 in F and F(n) can be arbitrarily large! Of course if all distinct, but could just play it safer by having 442 instead of 443.)

Hah, I was about to edit my comment to remark on me realising that this assumes 1 is valid! I agree, this is a bit of a dodgy case. EDIT: Ah, wait, I believe we do have equality of maxima (in fact, equality of $F_{>1}(n)$ and $f(n)$!) when we force the $a_i$ to all be strictly greater than $1$! Let $P(n):=\{p\in\mathbb{P}:p\mid n\}=\{p_1,\,\dots,\,p_t\}$ be the set of prime divisors of $n$ and define $\ell_p:=\max\{l:p^l\leq n\}$, $f(n)=\sum_{p\in P(n)}p^{\ell_p}$ as in the problem. Take any admissible tuple $(a_1,\,\dots,\,a_t)$ with all $a_i>1$ and let $S_i:=\{p\in P(n):p\mid a_i\}$. Each $S_i$ is non-empty, and since the $a_i$ are pairwise coprime, the $S_i$ are pairwise disjoint, hence $$t\leq\sum_{i=1}^{t}\left|S_i\right|\leq\left|P(n)\right|=t.$$ Therefore, each $S_i$ has size $1$, so after reordering, $a_i=p_i^{e_i}$ for some $e_i\geq 1$. Since $a_i\leq n$, we have $e_i\leq\ell_{p_i}$, so $$\sum_{i=1}^{t}a_i\leq\sum_{i=1}^{t}p_i^{\ell_{p_i}}=f(n).$$ Thus, $F_{>1}(n)\leq f(n)$. The reverse inequality is indeed immediate since $(p_1^{\ell_{p_1}},\,\dots,\,p_t^{\ell_{p_t}})$ is an admissible tuple with all entries $>1$, and its sum is $f(n)$. So in fact, $F_{>1}(n)=f(n)$ meaning any counterexample to $\max_{n\leq x}F(n)=\max_{n\leq x}f(n)$ must have at least one $a_i=1$. In the original problem statement, there are exactly $t=\omega(n)$ of the $a_i$, so it doesn't seem likely to me that $a_i=0$ can be taken as a value (even ignoring it from the coprimality condition) in the intended sense of the problem. So, TL;DR, it is trivially true if $a_i>1$, not true for all $x$ if $a_i=1$ is allowed, or something is off about the problem statement itself if there are not to be exactly $t$ $a_i$, or if $0$ is to be allowed as a value, ignoring it from the coprimality condition.

Also, FWIW, something else to note: From $H(x)\ll x\log\log\log x$, one can show $f(n)=o(n\log\log n)$ for almost all $n$. Fix $\varepsilon>0$ and let $E_\varepsilon(x):=\{n<x:f(n)>\varepsilon n\log\log n\}$. The integers $n<\sqrt{x}$ contribute at most $\sqrt{x}=o(x)$, so it suffices to count bad $n\in[\sqrt{x},x)$. For such $n$, $$\log\log n\geq\log\log\sqrt{x}=\log\log x-\log 2=(1+o(1))\log\log x$$ uniformly as $x\to\infty$. Hence, for all sufficiently large $x$, $$\log\log n\geq\frac{1}{2}\log\log x\qquad(\sqrt{x}\leq n<x).$$ Therefore, every $n\in E_\varepsilon(x)\cap[\sqrt{x},x)$ contributes $$\frac{f(n)}{n}>\varepsilon\log\log n\geq\frac{\varepsilon}{2}\log\log x$$ to $H(x)$. Thus, $$\frac{\varepsilon}{2}\log\log x\cdot\left|E_\varepsilon(x)\cap[\sqrt{x},x)\right|\leq\sum_{n<x}\frac{f(n)}{n}=:H(x)\ll x\log\log\log x.$$ So, $$\left|E_\varepsilon(x)\cap[\sqrt{x},x)\right|\ll_\varepsilon x\frac{\log\log\log x}{\log\log x}=o(x).$$ Adding back the $O(\sqrt{x})$ discarded integers gives $\left|E_\varepsilon(x)\right|=o(x)$. Since this holds for every fixed $\varepsilon>0$, we obtain $f(n)=o(n\log\log n)$ for almost all $n$.

Aha yes, thanks. Interestingly in [Er84e] he wrote 'For almost all $n$ we probably have $\frac{F(n)-f(n)}{n}\to \infty$ (1) and perhaps even for almost all $n$\[f(n)=o(n\log\log n), F(n)> cn\log\log n\quad (2).\]I seem to have difficulties with proving (1) and the second inequality of (2)...' This suggests that he did know the first inequality of (2) was true (and indeed it follows easily from his estimate on $H(x)$ in that same paper as you note), although why he didn't make this clearer is strange.

Looking again at [Er84e], indeed the statement is a little wrong - there is no restriction on the number of summands of $F(n)$ (he doesn't mention $1$, but I assume he was thinking of the $a_i$ as distinct, so it doesn't make much difference). In which case your original construction is still a counterexample.
f(n) is A339378 (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
